summary: High-risk regulatory changes demand immediate attention: GCC has new requirements for general labeling, Halal food, mandatory expiration dates, and microbiological limits, all requiring verification against updated GSO standards. Singapore has a critical source data extraction failure for labeling, necessitating urgent manual review. Taiwan introduced new additive limits for GABA, expanded mandatory allergen labeling (including sulphite ≥10mg/kg), new pesticide residue limits, and revised nutrition claim regulations. Thailand has new prohibitions on certain foods. The United States added sesame as the 9th major food allergen, introduced new prohibited substances, set specific contaminant limits (e.g., Aflatoxins 20ppb, 3-MCPD 1ppm), and implemented extensive new labeling requirements for allergen preventive controls, declaration formats, specific allergen types (tree nuts, fish, shellfish), cochineal/carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, gluten-free claims, and sulfiting agents (≥10ppm). Moderate-risk changes include numerous additions across China, Codex, EU, GCC/GSO, India, Indonesia, and Japan for additive, contaminant, and microbiology limits, as well as general labeling. Several existing moderate rules were also removed, requiring verification of source changes or extraction issues.

changes.notes: General labelling requirements for prepackaged food, including but not limited to ingredient lists, nutritional information, net content, manufacturer details, and potentially allergen declarations, must be verified against the full text of this technical regulation.

General requirements for food to be considered Halal must be verified against the full text of this technical regulation. This impacts ingredients, processing, and potentially labelling if a Halal claim is made.
Mandatory expiration date requirements for food products must be verified against the full text of this technical regulation.
Specific microbiological limits for various foodstuffs and food ingredients must be verified against the full text of this technical regulation.

Specific use restrictions and labeling requirements for γ-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA) as a food ingredient must be verified.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish, and sulphite
changes.notes: Sulphite labeling is mandatory if the residue sulphur dioxide is 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food.
Food allergen labeling is required for prepackaged foods that contain any of the specified 11 allergens as an ingredient or food additive.
Specific limits for various pesticides in different food categories must be verified against the full standard.
Prepackaged food products must comply with revised regulations on nutrition claims.
Certain foods are prohibited from production, import, or sale. Verification of prohibited food lists is required.
Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) and labeled as 'PROHIBITED' or 'PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS'.

changes.newLimit: Milk, Eggs, Fish, Crustacean shellfish, Tree nuts, Peanuts, Wheat, Soybeans, Sesame
changes.notes: The FASTER Act (effective January 1, 2023) declared sesame as the 9th major food allergen. These allergens account for 90 percent of food allergies and serious allergic reactions in the U.S.
Guidance Level for 3-MCPD.
Action Level for total Aflatoxins.

changes.parameter: Chemical Contaminants (Tolerances, Action Levels, Guidance Levels)
changes.notes: Manufacturers must check the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool for specific tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels for poisonous or deleterious substances in human food. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 CFR parts 109 and 509.

The 'Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food' rule (CGMP & PC rule, 21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.
Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine must be declared on the label.
The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels.

The FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling. A separate rule (August 12, 2020) establishes compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the 'gluten-free' claim.

FALCPA requires foods or ingredients containing a 'major food allergen' to be specifically labeled with the name of the allergen source. This applies to most packaged food products, including dietary supplements.

The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label either in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., 'lecithin (soy)') or immediately after or next to the ingredient list in a 'contains' statement (e.g., 'Contains wheat, milk, and soy.').
The type of tree nut (e.g., almonds, pecans, walnuts), and the species of fish (e.g., bass, flounder, cod) and Crustacean shellfish (e.g., crab, lobster, shrimp) must be declared.
Sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food at a concentration of ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
The use of food additives must comply with the specified national standard. Specific limits and conditions are detailed within the standard.

changes.oldLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
changes.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. Users are encouraged to consult the online database and its Preamble for specific provisions.

changes.newLimit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and CCCF established levels
changes.notes: Codex establishes Maximum Levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities to protect human health, keeping levels as low as possible based on sound scientific evidence. The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses these permitted maximum levels or guideline levels.

Specific additive limits and permitted uses require verification from official regulations under 'วัตถุเจือปนอาหาร'.

Specific limits for contaminants, toxic substances, and veterinary drug residues require verification from official regulations. Draft announcements regarding these limits, including ASEAN MRLs, are under consideration.
General food labeling and nutrition labeling requirements must be verified from official regulations under 'การแสดงฉลากอาหารและฉลากโภชนาการ'.

Food additives and color additives must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively) or evaluated by FEMA/JECFA for flavoring substances.
GRAS substances must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184) or in the inventory of GRAS Notices.
Substances approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, must be listed in 21 CFR Part 181.
